comment: Reworded lead section so that it matches more with "Definition" section below - the fact is, there are no universally accepted definitions as to what a mountain truly is. If anyone has any better way of rewording the lead, feel free to do so, many thanks.
/* Lead */ "typically rising 300 metres (980 ft)" - added citation span template - mountains actually seem to lack a TYPICAL lower limit
A lot of higher mountains (above 1000-2000m) have a much higher prominence above the surrounding land than just 300m, so tweaked accordingly.
/* Lead */  photo "Everest North Face toward Base Camp Tibet Luca Galuzzi 2006.jpg" - size parameter - changed "upright=1.5" to "upright=1.2" - see [[WP:IMAGESIZE]]
Updated sentence structure and added clarification.
rm excess images that were crowding the article, move 1
/* Mountain societies and economies */ rm paragraph not specifically relevant to mountains
just a little change of tallest to highest to better fit the cambridge definitions of what high and tall mean. plus, i added a mount everest sidenote
Rescuing 4 sources and tagging 0 as dead.) #IABot (v2.0.9.5

Tweaked lead to reflect information below (in Definitions & Erosion sections); some plateaus/plateaux can also be considered mountains (or table mountains), especially if they rise high above the surrounding terrain with extremely steep sides.

/* Definition */  Some lower mountain ranges can be called hills or hill ranges (especially in the Himalayas) so given mention to that with two examples that are sourced as this was missing from this section.
/* Lead */ height - no supporting evidence that 610 m (2,000 m) is the typical minimum height

revert lead section to version as of November 27 (revision 1324374741 by GreenC bot); 'Template:Infobox mountain' is formatted for containing information about *individual* mountains, & its usage here just vaguely places "various" into the params, rather than usefully summarizing article information;  [[MOS:INFOBOXUSE]]
unnecessary extra lead image outside of infobox
Undid revision [[Special:Diff/1326001303|1326001303]] by [[Special:Contributions/Jamie Eilat|Jamie Eilat]] ([[User talk:Jamie Eilat|talk]])
RV [[WP:good faith|good faith]], 'Template:Infobox mountain' is for containing information about individual mountains, rather than the entire concept
Reformat 2 archive links. [[User:GreenC/WaybackMedic_2.5|Wayback Medic 2.5]] per [[WP:USURPURL]] and [[WP:JUDI#Bot_run_results|JUDI batch #29aa]]
/* Definition */ HTTP to HTTPS for [[United States Geological Survey|USGS]]

Mountains are not only found on Earth

/* Definition */ Mont Blanc is on the border between France and Italy (and not solely in Italy. Claims have been made from both sides but are still unresolved as of now 2025, and are unlikely to be anytime soon).
/* Mountains as sacred places */ believed biblically, not like, really.

Reworded lead so that it is more consistent with the "Definition" section below because the wording in the lead seemed to imply a commonly accepted definition for mountains even though the section below states there is no universally accepted definition for a mountain.
fix the "citation needed" error in the "mountain and human" section
/* See also */  add link to relevant page
Move link to summit article to first appearance of term. For isolated mountains, link to monadnock.
/* Mountains and humans */ 'death zone' is a popular term often used to promote books etc

/* Definition */ There are different classes of mountainous terrain listed in the PDF cited, and some classes were omitted or had incomplete information.

Fixed a typo found with [[Wikipedia:Typo_Team/moss]] and reduced ALLCAPS.  Please see [[Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Capital_letters#All_caps]].
Added images to bridge table of contents
Article seems adequately sourced at this point. Any remaining statements that seem dubious should be flagged individually.
/* Erosion */ Seems unnecessary to copy lead paragraph of erosion article. Erosion is a fairly familiar term being used in its ordinary sense.
Replace prominence requirement from self-published source with one from the AGi Glossary of Geology.

Per talk page discussion, added mention of mass wasting. Gave very rough time scale for mountain formation. Subsequent leveling really is very slow; Appalachians are still here after 300 million years. Clarify a bit on climate.
More precise definition of mountain for first paragraph, with supporting source.
